content.problemStatement:
Given a binary search tree, print its nodes in sorted order.

For eg., the nodes in sorted order of the given BST are: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7




Input Format

The first line of the input contains s single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary search tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on a single line NNN space separated integers - the node values of given BST in sorted order.
constraints: All node values are distinct and form a permutation of 1..N
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N — the number of nodes in the BST. The next N‑1 lines each contain three space‑separated values: pi (parent node value), ci (child node value) and Ri ('L' if ci is the left child of pi, otherwise 'R').
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers – the node values of the given BST in ascending order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree contains the values 1 through 7. Inorder traversal of any BST with these values yields the sorted sequence 1 2 3 4 5 6 7.
First test case: a single node with value 1, so output is "1". Second test case: nodes 1,2,3 form a BST; sorted order is "1 2 3".
Regardless of the shape, the node values are 1..5, therefore the sorted order is 1 2 3 4 5.

solution.approach: The values stored in a binary search tree are unique and satisfy the BST property, therefore an inorder traversal visits the nodes in non‑decreasing order. Instead of building the tree, we can simply collect every node value that appears (both parents and children), sort the collected values and print them. This works because the input guarantees that the set of values is exactly {1,…,N}. The algorithm for each test case is: 1) read N, 2) read the N‑1 edge lines, adding both pi and ci to a vector, 3) after reading all edges also add the root value (which will already be present, but adding again does not hurt), 4) sort the vector, 5) output the sorted values separated by spaces.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the node values.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        # skip N-1 edges (each edge has 3 tokens)
        for _ in range(N - 1):
            next(it); next(it); next(it)
        # values are 1..N
        out_lines.append(' '.join(str(i) for i in range(1, N + 1)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
